sentences.text: And I'm rolling.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]
OK, sir.
No.
As long as we did what we are supposed to do, they left us alone.
On the home scene, people were disappearing.
My cousin disappeared one-- one day.
We didn't know where he was, and so were other people disappearing.
We didn't know what-- where they were.
They were just grabbing people and taking them out of town and shooting them.
And that's it.
I didn't see them, but I know my cousin disappeared and never came back.
Take them outskirts of town and shoot them in the outskirts of town.
So there was a nice Jewish population there.
Oh, yes there was about 15,000 Jews there in Dubno.
About five to 10 a day.
No.
Just Jews.
And you could really feel the difference between being Jewish and not Jewish.
Mm-hm.
They were bad.
They sided up with the Germans right away.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were great in recognizing Jews, where the Germans couldn't recognize a Jew, who was a Jew and who wasn't.
So they-- they would go around with the Germans and point out this is a Jew and this is a Jew.
Mm-hm.
Ah, either they'd take him out and shoot him, or they send them away to concentration camp.
Gendarmarie.

Till the end of 1941, when-- when I saw that they were bringing in the Ukrainian police from-- from the outskirts, from the surrounding areas and getting ready for some actions, whatever they-- I told my cousin, I said, I don't like what's going on there.
I'm going to go run away.
Yes, my parents knew.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, they were home, and they couldn't go with me.
I didn't know what I was going to be, but being in the woods, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I encountered a Partisan group, and I-- I joined them.
I joined them.
I embraced my family and said goodbye.
And that's all.
No, I never saw them again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Mm-hm.
A lot of young Jewish kids, they ran away also.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No, we went with a group.
We had to have the-- the help from the Jewish police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a group of about 50 to 100 youngsters that were running away, and we couldn't just go out and run.
We had to make it look that we are going to work.
To the outskirts of town, they had a-- a factory of sausages that they used to make sausages there.
And-- and the Germans used to take a lot of people from-- from the ghetto to work there in-- in that factory.
So we-- we had to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] go in formation.
And the Jewish police escorted us.
And when it came on the outskirts of town, we had to go to the factory to the right.
We started going to the left.
We went to the left.
Then we ran into the woods.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
What happened-- when I worked for the gendarmerie they used to pick up weapons that the Russians threw away-- threw away.
And they used to store them in a second part of the garage.
So I told my cousin we better get a couple of rifles here before we go.
So I picked up a couple of rifles.
And I took-- I had a little two-wheeler with a boxed in box on top.
And I used to take home [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the sawings from the wood.
They used to allow me take it home.
Sawdust.
So and I-- I put a couple of rifles on one of those two-wheelers.
One-- the short one was OK.
But the long one, I couldn't get it.
It stuck out.
So I put some-- I put some rags around it, and I-- I went to the ghetto with it.
Yes.
I had two.
I hid them.

I hid them There were-- there were the-- also Jewish kids working-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a-- [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] in a military post, which they-- they took the guns and they cut it-- cut off half-- half of the pipe and a  piece of the wood.
They used to take them under their clothes and-- and bring them home.
So all-in-all, they had about 100 rifles and 100 guns like that.
No, we never had any experience what to-- how to maintain them.
No, I never shot a gun before.
I was scared.
We were in the woods.
And the people that were supposed to take care of us, all they told us-- we're going to go to a-- a number 15 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] section.
We're going to go to number 15 section.
So we got to the woods.
And it got night, and everybody wanted to sleep.
We fell asleep.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We fell asleep, and in the morning we got up.
All those fellows that were supposed to take us to section 15 weren't there anymore, and the guns weren't there anymore.
So we had to do the best we could.
Well, we-- we watched the road, and when we saw some farmer going by there, we ran out, and we tried to buy some food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of those guys said, you don't have to buy anything.
You wait here, and I'll buy you-- I'll bring you some food.
He brought a-- a bucket full of soup.
And we had that soup and existed the best way we could.
We went on the fields and stole some potatoes and carrots from the fields and fed ourselves with that.
Then that wasn't enough for these Ukrainians, the Banderovcis, that wasn't enough.
So they kept attacking us in the woods when we were in the woods.
The Banderovcis were the Ukrainians.
They were fighting for an independent Ukraine.
And they were sort of like Ukrainian Partisans.
Yeah, they were Ukrainian Partisans, but for their own cause, to-- to get a country of their own.
And they were still fighting against the Germans.
Yes.
The Bulbovcis-- they were the same kind of group, but different leaders.
The Bulbovcis had  a different leader, and the Banderovcis had a different leader.
They were two different groups, but they were all Ukrainians, and they were all trying to clear out the area free of Jews.
And they kept on attacking us in the woods.
So you're fighting a war against the Ukrainians and the Germans.
Yes.
Well, like  I said, to begin with, we had about 50-- 50 guns with us.
At a later time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met-- I met one leader also from Polish resistance, and he-- he-- he knew my father, and he-- he-- he knew my younger brother.
And I told him about it.
So he called in one of the fellows that were with him.
Shuhart, come here.
Show him that gun.
So he came in with my gun.
I said, that's my gun.
He said, give him-- give him back the gun.
So he gave me back my gun.
And then we were on our own already.
I was more or less the leader there.
Mm-hm.
The nickname came later when I was in the Partisans.
You're in the woods, and you're dealing with the elements.
You're trying to stay alive, but you're not necessarily involved in any resistance movements.
You're just trying to stay alive.
Not yet.
We just tried to stay alive.
Well, they attacked our camp.
The Ukrainians attacked our camp.
And the two fellows that were left behind watching the camp-- they killed those two fellows.
Mm-hm.
So I said to the fellows, this is-- this is no good.
We got to do something about it.
We got to organize a stronger-- a stronger group and let's attack them.
Instead of they attacking us, let's attack them.
So we found out where they were keeping themselves, and we attacked them.
We went into the village where they were-- where they were stationed there.
And we found out in what house they were housed, and we surrounded the house.
We start telling them, come out.
Put your hands up or we-- we're going to burn you out of there.
If-- if they came out.
We didn't-- we didn't shoot there.
We just-- we just had a little--
Conversation.
Conversation, yeah.
Yes.
Well, in one camp, six of my people got killed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They just came, and they start shooting at our camp without any warning.
We were fighting back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After this was all going on, there-- there was a Russian Partisan group over there formed itself.
And the local population reported to the Russians that there is a Jewish group in your Partisans.
So one of the Russian leaders came over, and he asked him if he want to join them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I and another fellow, Yoste Shuhart, joined them.
And we were now under the Russian Partisans under the Russian command.
Whatever they had to do, we did it.
So now you're officially unofficially a Partisan with the Russians?
Mm-hm.
So we were blowing up bridges and blowing up [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] railroads that they were sending [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the front, supplies to the front.
So we were blowing it up.
No, we didn't have to learn because it was already-- all they had to do was pull a string and that ignited the--
Explosives.
But the explosives were ready.
All you had to do was pull a string to ignite it.
So we picked a-- we picked a-- a spot where it would do the best damage for-- for the railroads.
And we went there.
We dug this under the railroad tracks.
And we stood back about 5,000 feet when-- when we heard a train going by that area.
So we pulled the string and blew up-- blew up the track and the train.
Yes, it felt very good.
It was dangerous work.
That's how I got my-- my nickname, Nieustrazony Rozpoznawczy, because I did not fear any-- anything they were sending me out to do, I went with pleasure to do it.
I got my nickname Nieustrazony Rozpoznawczy.
That means fearless reconnaissance, fearless reconnaissance.
Well, when you do it over and over again, you get used to it like anything else.
Not-- not-- not for the sake of shooting.
I shoot-- I shot some people in a battle, in a war, like the Banderovcis.
They-- they surrounded us, and they wanted to chase us out of that area where we were-- where we were stationed, we had our camp.
And we had only about 200 people at one time.
They surrounded us with 600 people strong.
So we were fighting them.
And we made our way out of that--
That area.
And we went to a different area.
It was called Turemne, Turemne.
T-U-R-E-M-N-E.
Turemne.
There was a woods where they had-- we had water.
That was a water spout coming there.
So we had water there, and we kept our camp there.
Then we had another camp for the winter.
We had pine trees in that camp for the winter because you could-- with an airplane you could see, you know, the Germans used to fly over that area.
And when they see something going on there, they used to throw bombs.
So we-- in the wintertime, we had to go to different camp.
So you're busy trying to inflict as much damage--
Trying to survive and-- and do the most damage we-- we could do.
Yes.
And they would say here--
They would say here is a good spot where to blow up the train because it was a curving area, where the-- where the train had to curve.
And that's usually a good area because when you blow it up, it comes-- pulls all the rest of the--
Cars with it.
If it was ammunition there, we took the ammunition with us.
Sometimes there were troops.
They shot back at us, and we had a battle on our hands.
That's what it us.
And so you-- I mean, you were involved in basically trying to eliminate them and shooting them.
Mm-hm, that's right.
Not hand-to-hand, no.
I lost quite a-- a number of-- of good people there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
About 200 miles.
No, we had wagons and horses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Horses and wagons-- we moved around with horses and wagons.
No, we didn't hear anything on the camps.
So you were basically kind of cut off from the outside world.
Yes, we were cut off from the outside world.
Yes, we heard some stories.
Because they were coming with trucks and loading up the-- the peoples that  were in ghetto, taking them out to the airport, where they were digging-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Ditches.
They-- they-- they dug out ditches, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10 feet deep and 20 feet wide and 30 feet long.
And when-- when we still were in the ghetto, we inquired about these ditches.
So they said they were going to store potatoes in them for the winter.
That's what they told us.
In the meantime, they used it to shoot people.
They used it to shoot the population in there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
At least one a week.
At least one a week.
We had to do some reconnaissance and find out at what times they're going by a certain area.
And I was pretty good at it.
So I usually used to go and get the reconnaissance, how many trains go by a certain area where it was beneficial for us to-- to blow up that area.
Local population.
No, we didn't have any infiltrators.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I didn't-- I-- I was not afraid of being killed.
Because all my family was killed already.
I didn't have nothing to live for.
So I was not afraid of being killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So basically, until liberation, you were doing this kind of work.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Once a week, you'd figure out where to blow up a train.
At least once a week we-- we'd pick a target.
And then we-- we go out to that area and scout it out, which way was the best way to approach it and where-- where-- which area to blow it up.
By the time they-- they caught on, we were far away from that area because we used to change camps very often.
About every three or four days, we used to change camps.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
We have four minutes left on this tape.
Well, we picked areas where-- where water was close by and where we-- we wouldn't be spotted from the air.
So we picked areas like that where it was water close by.
Everybody had a job to do.
Yours was blowing up the trains.
Mm-hmm.
To assemble food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- we had to spot-- spot it out where-- where we could get some food for 200 people.
No.
Ammunitions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
About 20 carloads.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
